notes: Yumemi Denshi, Kyoto (1993) · SHRINE-KEEPING / EPISTOLARY. Four seasons, a neglected hilltop shrine, letters from something unseen. Designed by Sachiko Ono. The Ono–Rocha correspondence (1991–) is why Fey Faire's roster crosses the Pacific; this game is Ono's half of that conversation.

shelf.label_subtitle: 羽の庭 · the feather garden · four seasons, one shrine
shelf.hardware: Yumemi 'Yume-kan' stick · Slowstick-compatible via the grey-import shim
shelf.shelf_condition: 1995 US grey import · a photocopied fan translation folded into the case, annotated in two hands
shelf.prior_owner: Olaf · the fan translation came with it
shelf.provenance_note: One of the two annotating hands on the fan translation is Amélie Rocha's. Her note against the fourth letter's English sentence reads: 'she spelled it like that on purpose. ask her yourself.' Nothing else in either hand explains it.
shelf.cover_blurb: A shrine on a hill has been alone too long. Sweep. Mend. Offer. Sit. Something is reading what you leave on the shelf, and once a season, it writes back.
back_of_case: · Four verbs. SWEEP · MEND · OFFER · SIT.
· Twenty offerable things. What you give it is the language.
· One letter per season. Written to you. Composed FROM you.
(grey import · fan translation included · two hands)
shelf.in_fiction_dev_note: Sachiko Ono founded Yumemi Denshi ('dream-seeing electronics') as the Pacific's answer to Oneironautics' Greek. She and Amélie Rocha corresponded from 1991 about whether the numinous localizes. The Faire travels; the shrine stays; both are visited.
shelf.meta_note: The fourth letter contains, regardless of lexicon, one sentence in English, uncommented: 'your carrnival is known here also.' — with the doubled r. The catalog's loudest unexplained object.

lore_tokens_conditional.hnn_gold_feather: the blank letter · the only letter that says everything · consumed by Fey Faire (Moth's solo line)
